title: Radioactive Decay
subtitle: Random, spontaneous emission from unstable nuclei

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Radioactive decay is…
options: Caused by high temperatures breaking apart the nucleus
A random, spontaneous process not affected by external conditions
Triggered by chemical reactions in the substance
Only possible in man-made radioactive materials
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Radioactive decay is random and spontaneous. It cannot be sped up or slowed down by heating, cooling, or chemical changes. It occurs in both natural and man-made materials.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Radioactive decay is a random, spontaneous process — it cannot be controlled by temperature, pressure, or chemical state.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.4.2 — Radioactive decay

steps.title: Radioactive Decay
steps.bodyHTML: Radioactive decay occurs when an unstable nucleus emits radiation to become more stable. The process is completely random and spontaneous — it is impossible to predict exactly when any individual nucleus will decay. Decay is not affected by the chemical state of the substance, its temperature, or any other external physical condition.
commonErrors: Thinking decay can be speeded up by heating the substance
Confusing radioactive decay with chemical decomposition
Assuming all atoms of a radioactive substance decay at once

aqa.questionText: A student claims that heating a radioactive sample will make it decay faster. Explain why the student is incorrect.

steps.label: State that decay is random and spontaneous
steps.content: Radioactive decay is a random, spontaneous nuclear process.

steps.label: Explain what affects decay
steps.content: Decay depends only on the stability of the nucleus, not on conditions outside the atom.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — nuclear process unaffected by external conditions

steps.label: Conclude
steps.content: Because heating is a physical/chemical change outside the nucleus, it has no effect on the rate of radioactive decay.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct conclusion linked to the nuclear origin of decay

aqa.finalAnswer: Decay is random and spontaneous; temperature cannot affect the nucleus, so heating has no effect on the decay rate.

aqa.questionText: Which statement best describes radioactive decay?

options.label: A
options.text: It is predictable and can be controlled by changing temperature

options.label: B
options.text: It only occurs in atoms that have been exposed to radiation

options.label: C
options.text: It is a random process where unstable nuclei emit radiation to become more stable

options.label: D
options.text: It speeds up when the radioactive substance is dissolved in water

markSchemeSteps: Decay is random and spontaneous — unstable nuclei emit radiation to become more stable ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Options A and D both suggest external conditions affect decay — they do not, as decay is a nuclear process.
